flashcards.front: What is the Synthetic Camera Model in computer graphics?
flashcards.back: A mathematical model that simulates a real camera to capture 3D scenes and project them onto a 2D display. It defines how 3D points are transformed into 2D image coordinates.

flashcards.front: What are the three fundamental transformations in the graphics pipeline?
flashcards.back: 1. Model transformation (object to world) 2. View transformation (world to camera) 3. Projection transformation (camera to image plane)

flashcards.front: Define View Volume in synthetic camera model.
flashcards.back: The 3D region in space that is visible through the camera. It is bounded by the near plane and far plane, defining what gets rendered.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between orthographic and perspective projection?
flashcards.back: Perspective: parallel lines converge at vanishing points, objects far away appear smaller. Orthographic: parallel lines remain parallel, same-size objects appear equal regardless of distance.

flashcards.front: What is Field of View (FOV) in camera model?
flashcards.back: The angular extent of the scene visible to the camera, typically measured in degrees. Larger FOV shows more of the scene with more distortion at edges.
A right-handed coordinate system where: X-axis points right, Y-axis points up, Z-axis points towards the scene (opposite to viewing direction).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the near plane (clipping plane)?
flashcards.back: The closest plane to the camera where rendering begins. Objects nearer than the near plane are clipped (not rendered) to prevent division by zero in perspective projection.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the far plane?
flashcards.back: The farthest plane from the camera where rendering ends. Objects beyond this plane are clipped and not displayed.
A pyramidal (perspective) or cuboidal (orthographic) volume that defines the region of 3D space visible to the camera. It is bounded by near and far planes and the four side planes.

flashcards.front: What is the aspect ratio in camera model?
flashcards.back: The ratio of image width to height (width/height). It ensures the rendered scene maintains correct proportions to match the display window.

flashcards.front: Define camera position in synthetic camera model.
flashcards.back: The point in world coordinates from which the camera views the scene. Also called the camera eye or camera location.

flashcards.front: What is camera orientation determined by?
flashcards.back: It is determined by three vectors: look-at vector (gaze direction), up vector (defines camera's up direction), and right vector (perpendicular to look and up).

flashcards.front: What is perspective divide in projection?
flashcards.back: The step in perspective projection where coordinates are divided by w (homogeneous coordinate), causing objects to appear smaller with distance.

flashcards.front: What is the viewing transformation matrix?
flashcards.back: A 4x4 matrix that transforms world coordinates to camera coordinates. It positions and orients the camera in the scene.

flashcards.front: Why is clipping performed before projection?
flashcards.back: Clipping removes geometry outside the view volume before projection, saving computation and preventing errors like division by zero with points behind the camera.
